questions.prompt: What does p hold after this code?
questions.explanation: &x means 'address of x'. So p is a pointer that stores the memory address where x lives, not the value 42.
questions.title: What a pointer holds

questions.explanation: *p dereferences the pointer - it reads the value at the stored address. Since p points to x, *p is 42.
questions.title: Dereference a pointer

questions.explanation: *p = 25 writes 25 to the location p points to, which is x. So x becomes 25 and is printed.
questions.title: Change value via pointer

questions.prompt: In int* p = &x;, what does the * mean here?
choices: It declares p as a pointer to an int
It makes a copy of x
questions.explanation: In a declaration, int* means 'pointer to int'. The same * symbol means dereference when used on an existing pointer (like *p), which is a common source of confusion.
questions.title: Meaning of int*

questions.explanation: The pointer first stores the address of a, then p = &b replaces that address. Dereferencing p now reads b, so the program prints 9. Reassigning the pointer does not change a.
questions.title: Point somewhere new

questions.prompt: What is wrong with this code?
choices: nullptr contains the number 0, so it safely prints 0
Dereferencing a null pointer causes undefined behavior
Pointers cannot be printed with cout
p must be declared as double*
questions.explanation: nullptr deliberately points to no object. Dereferencing it asks for a value at an invalid location, which causes undefined behavior and often crashes. Check that a pointer is not null before using *p.
questions.title: Do not dereference null
Pointers practice 7
questions.prompt: A pointer `score` is `nullptr`. What does `if (score != nullptr) { std::cout << *score; }` do?
choices: Nothing prints, because the guard is false and the body is skipped
It prints 0, because nullptr holds the value zero
It crashes, because *score runs before the check
It fails to compile, because you cannot compare a pointer to nullptr
questions.explanation: `score` is null, so `score != nullptr` is false and the block is skipped. Nothing is dereferenced and nothing prints. That is the whole point of the guard: it reaches `*score` only when there is a real object to read. Comparing a pointer to nullptr is normal and compiles fine.

questions.title: Pointers concept check 1
questions.prompt: Which warning about Pointers should a student remember?
choices: An uninitialized or null pointer does not identify a valid object. Dereferencing it has undefined behavior and can crash the program.
questions.explanation: A pointer holds an address. `&value` obtains an address, `*pointer` dereferences it, and `nullptr` represents no object. That reasoning supports the answer: An uninitialized or null pointer does not identify a valid object. Dereferencing it has undefined behavior and can crash the program.

questions.title: Pointers concept check 2
questions.prompt: Which choice describes a reliable Pointers practice?
questions.explanation: A pointer holds an address. `&value` obtains an address, `*pointer` dereferences it, and `nullptr` represents no object. That reasoning supports the answer: Initialize every pointer.

questions.title: Pointers concept check 3
questions.prompt: Which recommendation belongs in a review of Pointers code?
questions.explanation: A pointer holds an address. `&value` obtains an address, `*pointer` dereferences it, and `nullptr` represents no object. That reasoning supports the answer: Use nullptr for no target.
